title: Exposing ports on an internal network
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

I'm trying to deny internet / network access while exposing some ports.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. `sudo podman network create --internal internal-net`
2. `sudo podman run --network=internal-net -dt -p 8080:80 docker.io/library/httpd`
3. `sudo podman run -dt -p 9080:80 docker.io/library/httpd`
4. `curl localhost:8080`
5. `curl localhost:9080`

**Describe the results you received:**

localhost:9080 is accessible, localhost:8080 fails: 
`curl: (7) Failed to connect to localhost port 8080: Connection refused`

**Describe the results you expected:**
localhost:8080 should also be accessible

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**


**Output of `podman version`:**

```
podman version 3.0.1
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**
(ran as rooot)

comments.body: @Luap99 @mheon PTAL

First question is, does this work with Docker? To me, internal means
internal and an internal network should not have any access to the outside.

From a technical perspective, this also sounds completely out of what CNI
offers now. We’d either need a rewritten version of the Portmap plugin, or
to use slurp.

On Sat, Jun 19, 2021 at 05:22 Daniel J Walsh ***@***.***>
wrote:

> @Luap99 <https://github.com/Luap99> @mheon <https://github.com/mheon> PTAL
>
> —
> You are receiving this because you were mentioned.
>
>
> Reply to this email directly, view it on GitHub
> <https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/10729#issuecomment-864381082>,
> or unsubscribe
> <https://github.com/notifications/unsubscribe-auth/AB3AOCDMBIWEFNR5AW7NK6LTTROUJANCNFSM4662DA5Q>
> .
>

@Roxolasu I do achieve the same by running iptables in createContainer OCI hook. This works most of the time in rootless mode with Slirp4netns. However I decided to add new "netwprksetup" extension hook exactly for that purpose (see #10704). If you run containers in non-root mode, jailed process won't be able to alter iptables rules within the network namespace.

If you need an example of how I do it, let me know.﻿
@basilgello An example would be much appreciated!

@Roxolasu here you go:

First, you define the following the script in `/etc/containers/restrict-outbound-access` and `chmod 0755` it:

```shell
#!/bin/sh

IPTABLES=$(command -v iptables)
IP6TABLES=$(command -v ip6tables)

if [ -z "$IPTABLES" ] || [ -z "$IP6TABLES" ]; then
	[ -x "/sbin/iptables" ] && IPTABLES="/sbin/iptables"
	[ -x "/usr/sbin/iptables" ] && IPTABLES="/usr/sbin/iptables"

	[ -x "/sbin/ip6tables" ] && IP6TABLES="/sbin/ip6tables"
	[ -x "/usr/sbin/ip6tables" ] && IP6TABLES="/usr/sbin/ip6tables"
fi

while read _1
do
	"$IPTABLES" -A OUTPUT -d "$_1" -j ACCEPT
done <<.p
$(LC_ALL=C ip addr | awk '
$1 == "inet" && !($2 ~ /^127./) {
	sub(/\/.+/, "", $2)
	ips[$2]=1
}

END {
	for (ip in ips) {
		print ip
	}
}
')
.p

"$IPTABLES" -A OUTPUT -j DROP
```

It allows outgoing traffic only to Slirp4netns gateway to be able to forward traffic between your mapped ports and jailed app but nothing more.

Then, define an OCI hook in `/etc/containers/oci/hooks.d/restrict-outbound-access.json`:

```json
{
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "hook": {
    "path": "/etc/containers/restrict-outbound-access"
  },
  "when": {
    "annotations": {
      "restrict-outbound-access": "true"
    }
  },
  "stages": ["createContainer"]
}

```

Finally, as root start the Busybox official container, for example:

```shell
podman run \
	--rm -it \
	--annotation "restrict-outbound-access=true" \
	--subuidname roxolasu --subgidname roxolasu --user 1000 \
	--network slirp4netns -p 8081:8080 \
	docker.io/library/busybox \
	/bin/sh
```

and inside the container, check that nothing is accessible:

```shell
busybox wget http://104.18.7.156
```

but you can start busybox httpd on port 8080:

```shell
cd /tmp
echo w00f > /tmp/index.html
busybox httpd -v -f -p 8080
```

then detach from container pressing Ctrl+P followed by Ctrl+Q and check the server replies with "w00f" on host port 8081:

```shell
curl -v http://ip:8081
...
w00f
```
﻿
@basilgello Wow, thank you!
can we close this?
Reopen if you believe this still needs to be fixed.
It would certainly be nice to have a simple and default way to prevent outbound access from a container. This might be better suited for a new feature request. What would you prefer, if any?

You want to prevent a container from using the network?

--net=none

Or do you want something else?

I want to prevent a container from using the network *while having exposed ports*.

The host should be able to access the exposed ports, the container should be prevent from accessing any outside its pod.

If the pod must be accessed, too, then create a private network and attach to it. My solution is for containers that need to have Internet-only connectivity without letting them reach infrastructure :)
Either macvlan or `--network=private` or `--network=container:id`

> @Roxolasu here you go:
> 
> First, you define the following the script in `/etc/containers/restrict-outbound-access` and `chmod 0755` it:
> ...


Thanks. 
This can work only for privileged users that are able to manipulate with iptables, right? ( Has `roxolasu` user these permissions ? )
I am trying to use `--userns=auto`, but getting `Error: OCI runtime error: error executing hook /etc/containers/restrict-outbound-access (exit code: 4)`
yes a unpriv user is not allowed to modify iptables.

@rhatdan is there any workaround? (the bridge is a no-go for me as these (rootless containers) should be completely isolated in terms of network connectivity ) 

Seems that OCI hook can not be triggered as another user ( root )
Did you run as root with --userns=auto and it failed?

Yes, I believe because of this `Fatal: can't open lock file /run/xtables.lock: Permission denied`.

EDIT:
Its working with `iptables-nft`, as it does not require /run/xtables.lock locking file.
So it is not allowed to create /run/xtables.lock, because it is running in a user namespace?
